g.data-narration: This lesson covers the systematic six-step design procedure for combinational logic circuits in digital design.
g.text: Systematic Six-Step Approach for Combinational Logic Circuits

g.data-narration: Moving from problem to functional circuit requires structured steps that ensure correctness and optimize cost.
g.text: Moving from problem to functional circuit requires structured steps.
This ensures correctness, optimizes gates/chips, and minimizes cost.

g.data-narration: The six steps are: Problem Definition, Truth Table, Boolean Function, Minimization, Logic Diagram, and Verification.
Step one requires understanding specifications completely and identifying all inputs and outputs clearly.
g.text: Understand specifications completely. Identify inputs and outputs.
Define signal meanings (active-high/low) and possible values.
Door opens (O=1) only if Stopped (S=1) AND Level (L=1)

g.data-narration: Step two constructs a truth table by enumerating all input combinations and their corresponding outputs.
g.text: Complete enumeration of all input combinations and their outputs.
The truth table is the unambiguous specification for your circuit.

g.data-narration: Step three derives the Boolean expression from the truth table by identifying all minterms where output equals one.
g.text: Derive Boolean expression from truth table. Identify minterms (rows where output = 1).
Expression = logical OR (+) of all minterms (Sum-of-Products form).

g.data-narration: Step four uses Karnaugh Maps to minimize the Boolean function, reducing gates, cost, and delay.
g.text: Reduce number of gates and inputs using Karnaugh Maps (K-Maps).
Minimization = cheaper, faster, more reliable circuit.

g.data-narration: Step five translates the minimized Boolean expression into a circuit diagram using available logic gates.
g.text: Translate minimized Boolean expression into circuit with gates.
Choose gates based on availability (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR).

g.data-narration: Step six verifies the design using simulation tools to ensure all input combinations match the truth table.
g.text: Verify design before building. Use simulation tools (Logisim, Vivado, LTspice).
Check all input combinations match original truth table specifications.

g.data-narration: This example demonstrates designing a two-bit comparator that outputs one when two 2-bit numbers are equal.
g.text: Compare two 2-bit numbers (A1A0 vs B1B0). Output E=1 if equal.

g.data-narration: Key takeaway one: a systematic approach ensures correct and efficient combinational logic design every time.

g.text: Systematic approach ensures correct, efficient combinational logic
Truth table is definitive reference - immune to misinterpretation

g.data-narration: Key takeaway three: minimization reduces complexity and cost. Never skip verification using software tools.

g.text: Minimization reduces complexity, cost, power, and increases speed
Never skip verification - use software tools for complex circuits

g.data-narration: Master this procedure to design all combinational logic circuits from ALUs to CPU components.
g.text: Master the Procedure, Master Digital Design
From ALU design to CPU components - this foundational methodology enables
creating all combinational logic in modern digital systems.
